bundle_description: How OpenClaw's Telegram bot integration works — from incoming messages through agent dispatch and response delivery.

features.name: Telegram Bot Polling
features.description: Long-polling loop that listens for incoming Telegram updates, deserializes messages, and dispatches them to the handler pipeline.

features.name: Message Routing
features.description: Routes incoming messages to the appropriate handler based on command prefixes, conversation state, and message type.

features.name: Agent Dispatch
features.description: Invokes the agentic AI core to process user queries — manages prompt construction, LLM calls, tool execution loops, and response synthesis.

features.name: Conversation Memory
features.description: Maintains per-user conversation history with sliding window context, summary compression, and persistent storage.

features.name: Response Formatting
features.description: Transforms raw agent output into Telegram-compatible messages with Markdown formatting, inline keyboards, and media attachments.

call_chain.role: Initializes the bot with API token, sets up handler registry, and enters the async polling loop.
Performs a single long-poll cycle against the Telegram getUpdates API with a configurable timeout.
Deserializes a raw Update into a structured Message, extracts chat_id/user_id, and dispatches to the router.
Iterates through registered handlers to find the first matching handler for the update.
Sends a typing indicator to the chat while the agent processes the query.

telegram_bot_polling.explanation: The Telegram integration uses an async long-polling architecture. start_polling() enters an infinite loop calling _poll_updates(), which sends a GET request to Telegram's getUpdates endpoint. When updates arrive, _process_update() deserializes the JSON payload into a typed Message object. The message is passed to HandlerRegistry.match() which checks handlers in priority order — command handlers first, then regex-based, then a catch-all. Meanwhile, _send_typing_action() runs concurrently to show a typing indicator.

call_chain.role: Bridge between Telegram handler and the agent core — retrieves context, constructs prompt, invokes the agent.
Main ReAct agent loop — sends prompt to LLM, parses tool calls, executes tools, iterates until final answer.
Constructs the messages array from system prompt, conversation history, and current user input.
Executes a tool call by name, validates parameters, runs the function, formats the result.
Parses LLM response to extract either a final answer or structured tool call requests.
Converts agent response into Telegram MarkdownV2 with code blocks, links, and inline keyboards.

agent_dispatch.explanation: AgentBridge.handle_message() retrieves conversation history then calls AgentManager.invoke() which implements a ReAct-style loop. It calls _build_messages() to construct the prompt, sends it to the LLM, and _parse_response() checks for tool calls. If tools are requested, ToolExecutor.run() executes each one and appends results. The loop continues until a final text answer is produced. ResponseFormatter.to_telegram() converts the output into Telegram MarkdownV2.

call_chain.role: Retrieves conversation history for a user, applies sliding window, checks if summary compression is needed.
Loads raw conversation turns from SQLite, ordered by timestamp, with max-turns limit.
Summarizes older messages into a concise paragraph using a fast LLM call when history exceeds context window.
Persists a new turn to the store, updating turn counter and last-active timestamp.

conversation_memory.explanation: Conversation memory uses a sliding-window strategy backed by SQLite. get_context() calls _load_history() to fetch recent turns. If token count exceeds the budget, Summarizer.compress() sends older turns to a fast LLM to produce a summary. After the agent responds, append() persists both user and assistant turns for cross-session continuity.
